channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Water
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] “FortiBleed” credential theft now feeds active ransomware — and those FortiGate firewalls guard the remote access small water utilities depend on
item.description: CISA warns the FortiBleed campaign has harvested working administrator and VPN credentials from tens of thousands of internet-facing FortiGate firewalls and SSL-VPN gateways — tied to roughly 74,000 exposed Fortinet devices inside an operation that collected over 110 million credentials since mid-June. On 2 July researchers linked the stolen access directly to the INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware crews, with at least a dozen deployments traced to it. Water and wastewater utilities — many small, thinly staffed and reliant on vendor remote access — are acutely exposed: these appliances terminate the remote access and IT/OT segmentation that SCADA, treatment and pumping controls depend on. Because valid credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, no patch closes the gap and an intruder logs in looking like a legitimate operator — treat any exposed FortiGate fronting plant OT as already compromised. - CISA / DHS / SecurityWeek - CISA alert 18 Jun 2026 — DHS IoCs 2 Jul; ransomware linkage confirmed; ongoing

item.title: [Critical] “CitrixBleed” returns: NetScaler flaw exploited within 24 hours, leaking secrets from gateways that front utility remote access
item.description: Citrix patched CVE-2026-8451 on 30 June — an unauthenticated memory-overread in NetScaler ADC and Gateway appliances configured as a SAML identity provider — and researchers saw in-the-wild exploitation within 24 hours, aided by a same-day public proof-of-concept. Like the original CitrixBleed, it lets a remote attacker read appliance memory and harvest live session tokens, credentials and TLS keys. Larger utilities and their integrators often use NetScaler to terminate remote access into SCADA and business systems, so leaked secrets can hand an attacker authenticated entry toward control networks. Assume any internet-facing, unpatched NetScaler had its memory read after 1 July. - CVE-2026-8451 - Patched 30 Jun 2026 — exploited within 24h; ongoing

item.title: [High] Cal Water finds no OT activity after Iran-linked Handala claimed it could disrupt the water supply
item.description: California Water Service says its Mandiant-assisted investigation into a breach claimed by Iran-linked group Handala found no evidence of activity in its operational-technology environment, even though the actor asserted it could have disrupted water service but chose not to. Handala — widely believed to front Iranian government operations — leaked roughly 5 GB of data; analysts found personal information and signs a customer billing system and an internal application may have been touched. The pattern is familiar for the water sector: IT-side theft plus a loud OT-disruption claim for psychological effect. The prudent response is to confirm OT integrity independently, assume claimed capabilities until disproven, and treat it as reconnaissance signaling against water. - SecurityWeek / Mandiant - Reported 25 Jun 2026 — OT impact not found

item.title: [High] Iranian-affiliated actors keep hunting internet-exposed PLCs and HMIs across US water systems (CISA AA26-097A)
item.description: The CISA/FBI/NSA/EPA joint advisory AA26-097A remains the defining guidance for the water sector: IRGC-affiliated actors (the CyberAv3ngers pattern) continue to seek out internet-exposed programmable logic controllers and HMIs — notably Unitronics Vision-series and similar devices — often exploiting default or absent passwords rather than novel vulnerabilities. Impacts to date have been defacement and nuisance disruption rather than unsafe water, but the targeting is deliberate and ongoing. This is foundational context for the current wave of hacktivist claims against utilities (see Cal Water); the mitigations are basic and effective, which is exactly why unpatched exposure is inexcusable. - CISA / FBI / EPA (AA26-097A) - Advisory 7 Apr 2026 — guidance current

item.title: [High] WaterISAC and federal partners warn of a heightened threat environment and potential Iranian retaliation against water systems
item.description: WaterISAC issued an updated situation report on 9 July flagging a heightened threat environment and the potential for retaliation by Iranian-affiliated threat actors following U.S. strikes on Iran, and urging drinking-water and wastewater utilities to raise vigilance. It reinforces the standing CISA/FBI/EPA guidance (see AA26-097A) that low-sophistication actors probe internet-exposed control devices at small and mid-size utilities, where flat networks and default credentials remain common. No new confirmed compromise of a U.S. water system is tied to this warning, but geopolitical spikes have historically been followed by opportunistic defacements and nuisance intrusions against exposed HMIs and PLCs — so the prudent response is to shrink internet exposure and confirm monitoring now, not after an alert. - WaterISAC / CISA / EPA - Situation report updated 9 Jul 2026 — current

item.title: [High] CISA and FBI warn Russian FSB “Berserk Bear” actors are compromising exposed routers — a direct risk to small water utilities
item.description: On 13 July CISA, the FBI and NSA, with international partners, issued advisory AA26-194A warning that Russian FSB Center 16 actors — publicly tracked as Berserk Bear / Dragonfly — are opportunistically compromising poorly configured and end-of-life networking devices, chiefly internet-facing routers, across critical-infrastructure sectors. The advisory documents them harvesting configurations, abusing legacy SNMP and altering settings to hold long-term footholds. Water and wastewater systems are an acute case: many small and mid-size utilities run aging, internet-exposed routers with default credentials in front of flat SCADA networks, PLCs and lift-station telemetry, with little monitoring. A compromised edge router hands an adversary a quiet path toward the very control devices that dosing, pumping and treatment depend on. This is a second front alongside the Iranian-affiliated PLC targeting utilities already face — and the fixes are basic router hygiene. - CISA / FBI / NSA - Published 13 Jul 2026 — ongoing

item.title: [Critical] Actively-exploited Lantronix EDS5000 flaw hands attackers root on the serial-to-IP bridges that connect water RTUs and meters
item.description: CISA says CVE-2025-67038, a command-injection flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers, is being exploited in the wild and added it to the KEV catalog on 23 June (advisory ICSA-26-069-02). An unauthenticated attacker can inject OS commands via the username parameter and run them as root, fully controlling a device whose entire job is bridging legacy serial gear — PLCs, RTUs, meters and sensors — onto IP networks. Forescout, which disclosed it among 20 “BRIDGE:BREAK” issues, showed such bugs can manipulate sensor readings to hide dangerous conditions — a direct concern where those readings drive dosing or pump control. - CVE-2025-67038 - KEV-added 23 Jun 2026 — exploitation since 5 Apr

item.title: [High] CISA advisory flags vulnerabilities in OpenPLC v3, used in water/wastewater and energy control
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-190-01 (published 9 July) flags an authenticated arbitrary-file-write flaw in OpenPLC v3, an open-source PLC runtime deployed in water, wastewater and energy control. The legacy web-UI program-upload workflow stores an attacker-supplied filename and later uses it as the write destination without validating the path, letting an authenticated user write files anywhere the webserver process can — a path to code execution or tampering on the controller. Open-source and low-cost PLC platforms increasingly show up in small-utility and skid-mounted control, where budgets are tight and lifecycle patching is inconsistent — exactly the segment Iranian-affiliated actors have probed for exposed control devices. No in-wild exploitation is reported, but operators running OpenPLC should patch to the fixed version, keep it off the public internet, and put remote access behind a VPN. - CISA (ICSA-26-190-01) - Published 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Rockwell Automation patches flaws across Logix controllers, RSLinx and FactoryTalk used in water SCADA
item.description: Rockwell Automation released fixes for multiple vulnerabilities spanning its Logix controller family, RSLinx communications software and FactoryTalk suite — including a stack-based buffer overflow in RSLinx that could allow remote code execution. Allen-Bradley/Rockwell gear is pervasive in water and wastewater SCADA, from ControlLogix and CompactLogix processors to the FactoryTalk HMIs operators watch all day. None of the issues are reported exploited in the wild, but the affected components sit deep in plant control and engineering workflows, so patching should be scheduled into the next maintenance window with management-network isolation in the interim. - Rockwell / SecurityWeek - Reported Jun 2026

item.title: [Info] CISA now targets September 2026 for the final CIRCIA rule — 72-hour incident and 24-hour ransom-payment reporting ahead
item.description: CISA is expected to finalize the CIRCIA rule this fall, with reporting now targeted for around September 2026. Once effective, covered critical-infrastructure entities — water and wastewater utilities among them — would have to report substantial cyber incidents within 72 hours and ransom payments within 24 hours to CISA. For utilities that already juggle EPA expectations and state requirements, the practical work is building an incident-classification and reporting playbook now, so a real event does not become a missed federal deadline. Track the final rule text for exact covered-entity thresholds and definitions. - Federal News Network - Final rule targeted ~Sep 2026
